elements.sent-2411-002: Inexact proximal augmented Lagrangian methods (pALMs) are particularly appealing for tackling convex constrained optimization problems because of their elegant convergence properties and strong practical performance.
elements.sent-2411-003: To solve the associated pALM subproblems, efficient methods such as Newton-type methods are essential.
elements.sent-2411-004: Consequently, the effectiveness of the inexact pALM hinges on the error criteria used to control the inexactness when solving these subproblems.
elements.sent-2411-005: However, existing inexact pALMs either rely on absolute-type error criteria (which may complicate implementation by necessitating the pre-specification of an infinite sequence of error tolerance parameters) or require an additional correction step when using relative error criteria (which can potentially slow down the convergence of the pALM).
elements.sent-2411-006: To address this deficiency, this paper proposes ripALM, a relative-type inexact pALM, which can simplify practical implementation while preserving the appealing convergence properties of the classical absolute-type inexact pALM.
elements.sent-2411-007: We emphasize that ripALM is the first relative-type inexact version of the vanilla pALM with provable convergence guarantees.
elements.sent-2411-008: Numerical experiments on quadratically regularized optimal transport (OT) problems demonstrate the competitive efficiency of the proposed method compared to existing methods.
elements.sent-2411-009: As our analysis can be extended to a more general convex constrained problem setting, including other regularized OT problems, the proposed ripALM may provide broad applicability and has the potential to serve as a basic optimization tool.
elements.sent-2411-010: Constrained optimization, admitting excellent modeling power for real-world applications across a wide range of fields, including machine learning and data science, engineering, operations research, is a central area in optimization, especially in the current big-data era \cite{boyd2004convex, cui2021modern .}
elements.sent-2411-011: This paper is dedicated to solving the following linearly constrained convex optimization problem: \begin{equation}\label{eq:mainprob} \begin{aligned} \min_{\bm{x}\in\mathbb{R}^N} \quad f(\bm{x}), \quad \mathrm{s.t.} \quad A\bm{x} = \bm{b}, \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $f:\mathbb{R ^{N}\to\mathbb{R}\cup\{+\infty\}$ is a (possibly nonsmooth) proper closed convex function, $A\in \mathbb{R ^{M\times N}$ and $\bm{b}\in\mathbb{R ^{M}$ are given data.}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: While we focus on \eqref{eq:mainprob in this paper, we would like to mention that the inexact algorithmic framework along with the associated theory developed in this paper can be extended to a more general convex constrained optimization problem studied in \cite{es2013practical .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: The augmented Lagrangian method (ALM) is recognized as one of the most popular and effective methods for solving constrained optimization problems \cite{hestenes1969multiplier, powell1969method .}
elements.sent-2411-014: The essential component of the ALM for solving problem \eqref{eq:mainprob involves penalizing the linear constraint $A\bm{x =\bm{b}$ to derive the augmented Lagrangian function, defined as \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_\sigma^{\rm prim}(\bm{x}, \bm{y}) = f(\bm{x}) + \left\langle \bm{y}, A\bm{x} - \bm{b}\right\rangle + \frac{\sigma}{2}\left\lVert A\bm{x} - \bm{b}\right\rVert^2,\quad (\bm{x}, \bm{y})\in \mathbb{R}^N\times \mathbb{R}^M,\end{equation} where $\bm{y}\in \mathbb{R ^M$ is the Lagrangian multiplier associated with the linear constraint and $\sigma>0$ is a penalty parameter.}}}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}L_\sigma^{\rm prim}(x, y) = f(x) + \left\langle y, Ax - b\right\rangle + \frac{\sigma}{2}\left\lVert Ax - b\right\rVert^2,\quad (x, y)\in R^N\times R^M,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-015: Then, for a given sequence of penalty parameters $\{\sigma_k\}\subseteq \mathbb{R _{++}$ and an initial Lagrangian multiplier $\bm{y ^0\in\mathbb{R ^M$, the ALM iteratively performs the following steps: \begin{equation}\begin{cases} \bm{x}^{k+1}\in \arg\min\limits_{\bm{x}\in\mathbb{R}^N} \left\{\mathcal{L}_{\sigma_k}^{\rm prim}(\bm{x}, \bm{y}^k)\right\}, [5pt] \bm{y}^{k+1} = \bm{y}^k + \sigma_k \nabla_{\bm{y}}\mathcal{L}_{\sigma_k}^{\rm prim}(\bm{x}^{k+1}, \bm{y}^k) = \bm{y}^k + \sigma_k \left(A\bm{x}^{k+1} - \bm{b}\right), \end{cases}\end{equation} which consists of minimizing one ALM-subproblem to get $\bm{x ^{k+1}$ and one gradient ascent step (associated with $\mathcal{L _{\sigma_k ^{\rm prim}$) with step size $\sigma_k$ to get $\bm{y ^{k+1}$.}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation} x^{k+1}\in \arg\min\limits_{x\inR^N} \left\{L_{\sigma_k}^{\rm prim}(x, y^k)\right\}, [5pt] y^{k+1} = y^k + \sigma_k \nabla_{y}L_{\sigma_k}^{\rm prim}(x^{k+1}, y^k) = y^k + \sigma_k \left(Ax^{k+1} - b\right), \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-016: Since the augmented Lagrangian function $\mathcal{L _\sigma^{\rm prim (\cdot)$ is associated with the primal problem \eqref{eq:mainprob , the above ALM is usually named as a primal-based ALM.}}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Alternatively, one may also consider a dual-based ALM which is associated with the dual problem of \eqref{eq:mainprob given by (modulo a minus sign) \begin{equation}\label{eq:maindual} \min\limits_{\bm{y}\in\mathbb{R}^M} \quad f^*({A}^{\top}\bm{y}) - \bm{b}^{\top}\bm{y},\end{equation} where $f^*:\mathbb{R ^N\to\mathbb{R}\cup\{+\infty\}$ denotes the conjugate function of $f$ and $A^\top$ is the transpose of $A$.}}
elements.sent-2411-018: Then, given a penalty parameter $\sigma>0$, the augmented Lagrangian function associated with problem \eqref{eq:maindual is given by (see Section \ref{sec:ripALM for the detailed derivation) \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_\sigma^{\rm dual}(\bm{y}, \bm{x}) = -\bm{b}^{\top}\bm{y} + \frac{1}{2\sigma}\big\|\bm{x}+\sigma{A}^{\top}\bm{y}\big\|^2 - \frac{1}{2\sigma}\|\bm{x}\|^2 - \mathtt{M}_{\sigma f}\big(\bm{x}+\sigma{A}^{\top}\bm{y}\big), ~~ (\bm{x}, \bm{y})\in \mathbb{R}^N\times \mathbb{R}^M,\end{equation} where $\mathtt{M _{\sigma f (\bm{x ) := \min\limits_{\bm{y }\left\{f(\bm{y ) + \frac{1 {2\sigma}\|\bm{y - \bm{x}\|^2\right\}$ denotes the Moreau envelope of the function $\sigma f(\cdot)$ at $\bm{x}$.}}}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}L_\sigma^{\rm dual}(y, x) = -b^{\top}y + \frac{1}{2\sigma}\big\|x+\sigma{A}^{\top}y\big\|^2 - \frac{1}{2\sigma}\|x\|^2 - M_{\sigma f}\big(x+\sigma{A}^{\top}y\big), ~~ (x, y)\in R^N\times R^M,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-019: Consequently, for a given sequence of penalty parameters $\{\sigma_k\}\subseteq \mathbb{R _{++}$ and an initial point $\bm{x ^0\in \mathbb{R ^N$, the dual-based ALM reads as follows: \begin{equation}\begin{cases} \bm{y}^{k+1}\in \arg\min\limits_{\bm{y}\in\mathbb{R}^M} \left\{\mathcal{L}_{\sigma_k}^{\rm dual}(\bm{y}, \bm{x}^k) \right\}, [5pt] \bm{x}^{k+1} = \bm{x}^k + \sigma_k \nabla_{\bm{x}}\mathcal{L}_{\sigma_k}^{\rm dual}(\bm{y}^{k+1}, \bm{x}^k) = prox_{\sigma_{k} f}\big(\bm{x}^k+\sigma_{k}{A}^{\top}\bm{y}^{k+1}\big). \end{cases}\end{equation} The choice between using a primal-based or dual-based approach is problem-dependent.}}}
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation} y^{k+1}\in \arg\min\limits_{y\inR^M} \left\{L_{\sigma_k}^{\rm dual}(y, x^k) \right\}, [5pt] x^{k+1} = x^k + \sigma_k \nabla_{x}L_{\sigma_k}^{\rm dual}(y^{k+1}, x^k) = prox_{\sigma_{k} f}\big(x^k+\sigma_{k}{A}^{\top}y^{k+1}\big). \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-020: For example, when $f$ is nonsmooth, but its proximal mapping is easy-to-compute, the dual-based ALM may be more preferable.
elements.sent-2411-021: This is because, in the dual-based ALM, the optimization problem for updating $\bm{y}$ involves minimizing a continuously differentiable objective function, which can substantially simplify the optimization process.
elements.sent-2411-022: The applicability of the ALM has been significantly expanded since Rockafellar's seminal works \cite{r1976augmented,r1976monotone , which established a deep connection between the ALM and the proximal point algorithm (PPA).}
elements.sent-2411-023: Moreover, the elegant convergence properties of the ALM can be established under the Lipschitz continuity of a certain solution mapping.
elements.sent-2411-024: Subsequent works have shown that this stringent condition can be further relaxed; see, for example, \cite{cui2019r,luque1984asymptotic and references therein.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Indeed, understanding the convergence properties of the ALM continues to be a topic of interest in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-026: Apart from theoretical advancements, numerous studies have demonstrated the remarkable efficiency of the ALM in solving various convex problems, including conic programming problems \cite{li2018qsdpnal,lst2020efficient,liang2021inexact,zst2010newton-cg , statistical optimization problems \cite{lst2018highly,lin2019efficient,zhang2020efficient , optimization problems in machine learning \cite{sun2021convex,yan2020efficient and image/signal processing \cite{li2013efficient,llm2019nonergodic , to mention just a few.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-027: These successful applications highlight the critical roles of both the solution methods used for solving ALM-subproblems and the error criteria that control the accuracy required for solving these subproblems, while preserving the appealing convergence properties of the ALM.
elements.sent-2411-028: To solve ALM-subproblems efficiently, second-order methods, such as Newton-type methods \cite{qi1993nonsmooth , are strong candidates due to their fast local convergence rates.}
elements.sent-2411-029: For example, once the iterate of a Newton-type method falls into the fast convergence region, only a few more iterations are needed to achieve a highly accurate solution.
elements.sent-2411-030: However, certain regularity conditions are needed to ensure the nonsingularity of the (generalized) Hessian of the ALM subproblem's objective function at its solution point.
elements.sent-2411-031: Theoretically, such regularity conditions are generally not verifiable in advance, except in some special cases \cite{liang2021inexact .}
elements.sent-2411-032: Numerically, ill-conditioned or singular (generalized) Hessians can lead to unpredictable numerical behaviors, resulting in unstable implementations and excessive efforts for parameter tuning.
elements.sent-2411-033: To enhance the applicability of a second-order method for solving ALM's subproblems, a common simple approach is to add a proximal term to the ALM subproblem's objective function, resulting in the proximal augmented Lagrangian method (pALM)\footnote{The pALM is also known as the proximal method of multipliers; see \cite{r1976augmented .. .}}
elements.sent-2411-034: Specifically, given $\{\tau_k\}\subseteq \mathbb{R _{++}$, $\{\sigma_k\}\subseteq \mathbb{R _{++}$ and $(\bm{x ^0, \bm{y ^0)\in \mathbb{R ^N\times \mathbb{R ^M$, the dual-based pALM iteratively performs the following steps: \begin{equation}\label{pALMscheme} \begin{cases} \bm{y}^{k+1} = \arg\min\limits_{\bm{y}\in\mathbb{R}^M} \Big\{\mathcal{L}_{\sigma_k}^{\rm dual}(\bm{y}, \bm{x}^k) + \cfrac{\tau_k}{2\sigma_k}\left\| \bm{y} - \bm{y}^k\right\|^2\Big\}, [8pt] \bm{x}^{k+1} = \bm{x}^k + \sigma_k \nabla_{\bm{x}}\mathcal{L}_{\sigma_k}^{\rm dual}(\bm{y}^{k+1}, \bm{x}^k) = prox_{\sigma_{k} f}\big(\bm{x}^k+\sigma_{k}{A}^{\top}\bm{y}^{k+1}\big). \end{cases}\end{equation} By incorporating a proximal term, the pALM is able to bypass the regularity conditions when applying a second-order method for solving its subproblem.}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-007: \begin{equation}\label{pALMscheme}  y^{k+1} = \arg\min\limits_{y\inR^M} \Big\{L_{\sigma_k}^{\rm dual}(y, x^k) + \cfrac{\tau_k}{2\sigma_k}\left\| y - y^k\right\|^2\Big\}, [8pt] x^{k+1} = x^k + \sigma_k \nabla_{x}L_{\sigma_k}^{\rm dual}(y^{k+1}, x^k) = prox_{\sigma_{k} f}\big(x^k+\sigma_{k}{A}^{\top}y^{k+1}\big). \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-035: This typically results in more robust implementations with reduced parameter tuning.
elements.sent-2411-036: We refer readers to \cite{de2021augmented,es2010proximal,hermans2019qpALM,hss2004some,lst2020asymptotically,liang2022qppal,lin2019efficient,lst2022augmented,pgk2024efficient,ylct2024corrected for successful applications of the pALM in addressing various important optimization problems.}
elements.sent-2411-037: To make the pALM truly implementable and practical, it must allow approximate solutions to the subproblem with progressively improved accuracy, and the associated error criterion must be practically verifiable, while preserving desirable convergence properties of the outer loop.
elements.sent-2411-038: To achieve this, an absolute-type error criterion was introduced by Rockafellar for the pALM in \cite{r1976augmented and is now widely used in the literature; see, for example, \cite{lst2020asymptotically,liang2022qppal,lin2019efficient,lst2022augmented,pgk2024efficient .}}
elements.sent-2411-039: However, this absolute-type error criterion requires the specification of a summable sequence of tolerance parameters, which must be tuned to avoid being too conservative or too aggressive.
elements.sent-2411-040: As a result, careful parameter tuning of this sequence is often necessary to achieve superior convergence performance.
elements.sent-2411-041: This process can result in excessive effort and potential inefficiencies in practical implementations.
elements.sent-2411-042: A notable alternative is to adopt a relative-type error criterion, which seeks to control the errors in minimizing the subproblems based on some quantity related to the progress of the algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-043: This approach eliminates the need for a summable tolerance sequence, thereby enhancing practical implementability.
elements.sent-2411-044: The adoption of a relative-type error criterion started from the seminal works of Solodov and Svaiter \cite{ss2000error,ss1999hybridap,ss1999hybridpr,ss2000inexact,ss2001unified on inexact versions of the PPA, and has since influenced the development of inexact versions of numerous algorithms, including the variable metric PPA \cite{pls2008class , ALM \cite{es2013practical , ADMM \cite{ey2018relative , FISTA \cite{bgk2023fista , and the Bregman-type methods \cite{yht2024inexact,yt2023inexact .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-045: However, to the best of our knowledge, research on relative-type inexact versions of the pALM remains in its early stage, with limited exploration in \cite{es2010proximal,hss2004some,ylct2024corrected .}
elements.sent-2411-046: In particular, all these inexact versions are derived by applying a (variable metric) hybrid proximal extragradient method \cite{pls2008class,ss1999hybridap,ss1999hybridpr to a certain primal-dual solution mapping of the convex constrained problem, and therefore require an additional correction step to ensure convergence.}
elements.sent-2411-047: Consequently, the algorithms developed in \cite{es2010proximal,hss2004some,ylct2024corrected essentially deviate from the vanilla pALM framework \eqref{pALMscheme .}}
elements.sent-2411-048: Moreover, our numerical results also indicate that the correction step tends to slow down the convergence of the pALM and thus increase the computational cost.
elements.sent-2411-049: These observations naturally lead us to raise the following question: \begin{tcolorbox [colback=black!0!white] Can we design a relative-type error criterion for the vanilla pALM, while preserving desirable convergence properties? \end{tcolorbox In light of this, the primary goal of this paper is to address the above question by designing a relative-type error criterion for the vanilla pALM \eqref{pALMscheme .}}}
elements.sent-2411-050: Note that the relative-type error criterion proposed by Eckstein and Silva \cite{es2013practical for the ALM innovatively introduces an auxiliary error variable to control the inexactness while ensuring convergence.}
elements.sent-2411-051: In this work, we shall demonstrate that a similar idea can be adapted to the vanilla pALM, but with essential modifications.
elements.sent-2411-052: We should emphasize that the corresponding theoretical convergence analysis also requires substantial modifications, due to the inclusion of the proximal term.
elements.sent-2411-053: Interestingly, while the initial motivation for considering pALM was to ensure the nonsingularity of the (generalized) Hessian of the ALM subproblem's objective function at its solution point, our analysis reveals that this proximal term can further improve the existing convergence properties of the relative-type inexact ALM studied in \cite{as2016note,es2013practical,zc2020linear .}
elements.sent-2411-054: Specifically, under some common assumptions, it ensures the convergence of both the primal and dual sequences, as well as their asymptotically Q-(super)linear convergence rates.
elements.sent-2411-055: The key contributions and findings of this paper are summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-056: We develop a relative-type inexact proximal augmented Lagrangian method (ripALM) to solve the dual problem \eqref{eq:maindual .}
elements.sent-2411-057: This proposed ripALM is the first inexact version of the vanilla pALM \eqref{pALMscheme .}
elements.sent-2411-058: It not only shares the same convergence properties for the sequence $\{\bm{x ^k\}$ as the relative-type inexact ALM studied in \cite{as2016note,es2013practical,zc2020linear , but also offers other theoretical advantages in that the sequence $\{\bm{y ^k\}$ is guaranteed to converge, and both primal and dual sequences achieve asymptotically Q-(super)linear convergence rates under a relatively weaker error bound assumption.}}}
elements.sent-2411-059: These results provide strong theoretical support for the applicability of ripALM, particularly in scenarios where accurate solutions of both primal and dual problems are required.
elements.sent-2411-060: Numerically, we implement the proposed ripALM and apply it to solve quadratically regularized optimal transport (QROT) problems.
elements.sent-2411-061: Extensive numerical results validate the promising performance of the proposed ripALM for solving large-scale QROT problems.
elements.sent-2411-062: Moreover, comparisons with existing methods underscore our motivation and contributions in developing a relative-type error criterion for the vanilla pALM.
elements.sent-2411-063: We also emphasize that our ripALM can be extended to solve optimal transport problems with other convex regularizers such as the group quadratic regularizer \cite{ylct2024corrected , provided that the regularizers are proximal-friendly and the generalized Jacobians associated with the pALM-subproblems are easy to compute.}
elements.sent-2411-064: Given the growing interests in developing efficient algorithmic frameworks for computational optimal transport \cite{pc2019computational , our work may open up new possibilities for applying the powerful pALM algorithmic framework in a wide variety of real-world applications.}
elements.sent-2411-065: The remaining parts of this paper are organized as follows.

elements.sent-2411-067: Section \ref{sec:app2qrot showcases how to apply the proposed ripALM for solving QROT problems.}
elements.sent-2411-068: Numerical experiments are conducted in Section \ref{sec:numexp .}
elements.sent-2411-069: Finally, some concluding remarks are summarized in Section \ref{sec:conclusions .. Notation.}

elements.sent-2411-083: In this section, we focus on developing a relative-type inexact proximal augmented Lagrangian method (ripALM) to solve the dual problem \eqref{eq:maindual .}
elements.sent-2411-084: The algorithmic framework is developed based on the parametric convex duality framework (see, for example, \cite{r1970convex,r1974conjugate and \cite[Chapter 11]{rw1998variational ).}}
elements.sent-2411-085: We first identify problem \eqref{eq:maindual with the following problem \begin{equation}\label{eq:para-proorg} \min\limits_{\bm{y}\in\mathbb{R}^M} \quad G(\bm{y}, \bm{0}),\end{equation} where $G:\mathbb{R ^{M}\times\mathbb{R ^{N}\to(-\infty,+\infty]$ is defined by \begin{equation}\label{eq:para-objpri} G(\bm{y},\bm{\xi}) := f^*({A}^{\top}\bm{y}+\bm{\xi}) - \bm{b}^{\top}\bm{y}.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-086: Note that $G$ is proper closed convex since $f^{*}$ is proper closed convex.
elements.sent-2411-087: Then, the (ordinary) Lagrangian function of problem \eqref{eq:maindual can be defined by taking the concave conjugate of $G$ with respect to its second argument (see \cite[Definition 11.45]{rw1998variational ), that is, \begin{equation}\label{eq:ripALM_lag} \ell(\bm{y},\bm{x}) := \inf\limits_{\bm{\xi}\in\mathbb{R}^N} \left\{G(\bm{y},\bm{\xi}) - \langle\bm{x}, \,\bm{\xi}\rangle\right\} = - \bm{b}^{\top}\bm{y} + \langle\bm{x},\,{A}^{\top}\bm{y}\rangle - f(\bm{x}).\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-088: Clearly, $\ell$ is convex in its first argument and concave in the second argument.
elements.sent-2411-089: For a given penalty parameter $\sigma>0$, the augmented Lagrangian function of problem \eqref{eq:maindual is defined by (see \cite[Example 11.57]{rw1998variational ) \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \mathcal{L}_{\sigma}(\bm{y},\,\bm{x}) := \sup\limits_{\bm{s}\in\mathbb{R}^N} \left\{\ell(\bm{y},\bm{s})-\frac{1}{2\sigma}\|\bm{s}-\bm{x}\|^2\right\} = -\bm{b}^{\top}\bm{y} + \frac{1}{2\sigma}\big\|\bm{x}+\sigma{A}^{\top}\bm{y}\big\|^2 - \frac{1}{2\sigma}\|\bm{x}\|^2 - \mathtt{M}_{\sigma f}\big(\bm{x}+\sigma{A}^{\top}\bm{y}\big). \end{aligned}\end{equation} From the property of the Moreau envelope (see \cite[Proposition 12.30]{bc2017convex ), we know that $\mathcal{L _{\sigma}$ is continuously differentiable with respect to its first argument and \begin{equation}\nabla_{y}\mathcal{L}_{\sigma}(\bm{y},\bm{x}) = A\mathtt{prox}_{\sigma f}\big(\bm{x}+\sigma A^{\top}\bm{y}\big) - \bm{b}.\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-090: With the above preparations, we are ready to present our ripALM for solving problem \eqref{eq:maindual in Algorithm \ref{algo:ripALM .}}
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{A relative-type inexact proximal augmented Lagrangian method (ripALM) for solving problem \eqref{eq:maindual}}\label{algo:ripALM} Input: $\rho\in[0,1)$, $\{\sigma_{k}\}_{k=0}^{\infty}$ is a sequence of real numbers such that $\inf_{k\geq0}\{\sigma_{k}\}>0$, and $\{\tau_{k}\}_{k=0}^{\infty}$ is a sequence of real numbers such that $\inf_{k\geq0}\{\tau_{k}\}>0$ and $\sup_{k\geq0}\{\tau_{k}\}<\infty$. Choose $\bm{y}^0,\,\bm{w}^0\in\mathbb{R}^M$ and $\bm{x}^0\in\mathbb{R}^N$ arbitrarily. Set $k=0$. while the termination criterion is not met, do \begin{itemize}[leftmargin=2.2cm] \item[Step 1.] Approximately solve the subproblem: \begin{equation}\label{ripALM-subpro} \min\limits_{\bm{y}\in\mathbb{R}^{M}}~~ \mathcal{L}_{\sigma_{k}}(\bm{y},\,\bm{x}^k) + \frac{\tau_{k}}{2\sigma_{k}}\big\|\bm{y}-\bm{y}^k\big\|^2 \end{equation} to find $\bm{y}^{k+1}\in\mathbb{R}^M$ such that \begin{equation}\label{ripALM-inexcond} \begin{aligned} \; 2\big|\langle\bm{w}^k-\bm{y}^{k+1}, \,\sigma_{k}\Delta^{k+1}\rangle\big| + \big\|\sigma_{k}\Delta^{k+1}\big\|^2 [3pt] \leq \; \rho\left( \big\|prox_{\sigma_{k} f}\big(\bm{x}^k+\sigma_{k}{A}^{\top}\bm{y}^{k+1}\big)-\bm{x}^{k}\big\|^2 + \tau_{k}\big\|\bm{y}^{k+1}-\bm{y}^k\big\|^2\right), \end{aligned} \end{equation} where $\Delta^{k+1} := \nabla_{y} \mathcal{L}_{\sigma_{k}}(\bm{y}^{k+1},\bm{x}^k) + \tau_{k}\sigma_{k}^{-1}(\bm{y}^{k+1}-\bm{y}^k)$. \item[Step 2.] Update \begin{equation}\label{ripALM_xwupdate} \bm{x}^{k+1} = prox_{\sigma_{k} f}\big(\bm{x}^k+\sigma_{k}{A}^{\top}\bm{y}^{k+1}\big), \quad \bm{w}^{k+1} = \bm{w}^k - \sigma_{k}\Delta^{k+1}. \end{equation} \item[Step 3.]Set $k = k+1$ and go to Step 1. \end{itemize} end while Output: $(\bm{y}^k,\bm{x}^k)$
elements.sent-2411-091: In line with pALM-type {methods , at each iteration, our ripALM in Algorithm \ref{algo:ripALM approximately minimizes the sum of the augmented Lagrangian function $\mathcal{L _{\sigma_{k (\cdot,\,\bm{x ^k)$ {and a proximal term $\frac{\tau_{k {2\sigma_{k }\big\|\cdot-\bm{y ^k\big\|^2$ under the error criterion \eqref{ripALM-inexcond , followed by the updates of the multiplier $\bm{x ^k$ and the {auxiliary error variable $\bm{w ^k$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-092: Due to the strong convexity of the objective function in \eqref{ripALM-subpro , the subproblem \eqref{ripALM-subpro has a unique solution, denoted as $\bm{y ^{k,*}$, satisfying $\Delta^{k,* :=\nabla_{y}\mathcal{L _{\sigma_{k (\bm{y ^{k,* ,\bm{x ^k) + \tau_{k}\sigma_{k ^{-1 (\bm{y ^{k,* -\bm{y ^k)=\bm{0}$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-093: Then, for any minimizing sequence $\{\bm{y ^{k,t}\}$ for \eqref{ripALM-subpro converging to $\bm{y ^{k,*}$, we have that $\Delta^{k,t :=\nabla_{y}\mathcal{L _{\sigma_{k (\bm{y ^{k,t ,\bm{x ^k) + \tau_{k}\sigma_{k ^{-1 (\bm{y ^{k,t -\bm{y ^k)\to \bm{0}$ and consequently $2\big|\langle\bm{w ^k-\bm{y ^{k,t , \,\sigma_{k}\Delta^{k,t}\rangle\big|+ \big\|\sigma_{k}\Delta^{k,t}\big\|^2\to 0$ as $t\to \infty$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-094: Meanwhile, if $\bm{y ^k$ is not the solution of \eqref{ripALM-subpro and $\tau_{k}\neq0$, the right-hand side of \eqref{ripALM-inexcond cannot {be zero.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-096: Compared to the recent semismooth Newton based inexact proximal augmented Lagrangian ({\sc Snipal ) method developed in \cite[Section 3]{lst2020asymptotically , our ripALM in Algorithm \ref{algo:ripALM employs a significantly different error criterion \eqref{ripALM-inexcond for solving the subproblem \eqref{ripALM-subpro .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-097: Specifically, in our context, {\sc Snipal requires the error term $\Delta^{k+1}$ to satisfy \begin{equation}\label{eq:inexact_SNIPAL} \begin{aligned} (A) ~~ \big\|\Delta^{k+1}\big\| \leq \frac{\min\left(\sqrt{\tau_{k}}, \,1\right)}{\sigma_k}\varepsilon_k, ~~ \varepsilon_k\geq0, ~~ \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \varepsilon_k<\infty, (B) ~~ \big\|\Delta^{k+1}\big\| \leq \frac{\delta_k\min\left(\sqrt{\tau_{k}}, \,1\right)}{\sigma_k}\sqrt{ \big\|\Delta_x^{k+1}\big\|^2 + \tau_{k}\big\|\Delta_y^{k+1}\big\|^2}, ~~0 \leq \delta_k<1, ~~\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \delta_k<\infty, \end{aligned}\end{equation} with $\Delta_x^{k+1 :=\bm{x ^{k+1 -\bm{x ^{k}$ and $\Delta_y^{k+1 :=\bm{y ^{k+1 -\bm{y ^k$, to guarantee an asymptotically Q-(super)linear convergence rate.\footnote{Note that the error criterion (A) in \eqref{eq:inexact_SNIPAL alone is sufficient for establishing the global convergence of the {\sc Snipal ; see \cite[Section 3]{lst2020asymptotically .}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-098: Both error criteria (A) and (B) in \eqref{eq:inexact_SNIPAL are of the absolute type, meaning that they require the pre-specification of two summable sequences of tolerance parameters, $\{\varepsilon_k\}\subseteq[0,\infty)$ and $\{\delta_k\}\subseteq[0,1)$, to control the error incurred in the inexact minimization of the subproblem.}
elements.sent-2411-099: Since there is generally no direct guidance on optimally selecting these tolerance parameters to achieve good convergence efficiency, this absolute-type criterion typically {requires careful tuning for the tolerance parameters, which may make {\sc Snipal less user-friendly in practical implementations.}}
elements.sent-2411-100: In contrast, the error criterion \eqref{ripALM-inexcond used in ripALM is of the relative type, meaning that the error $2\big|\langle\bm{w ^k-\bm{y ^{k+1 ,\,\sigma_{k}\Delta^{k+1}\rangle\big| + \big\|\sigma_{k}\Delta^{k+1}\big\|^2$ is regulated by a tentative successive difference related to the progress of the algorithm.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-101: Moreover, this relative-type criterion only involves a single tolerance parameter $\rho\in[0,1)$, thus simplifying the process of parameter tuning both computationally and in terms of implementation, as we shall see in subsection \ref{sec:comp_diff_ipALMs .}
elements.sent-2411-102: Thanks to the advantage of eliminating the need to select an infinite sequence of tolerance parameters, various versions of relative error criteria have been widely adopted in numerous well-known algorithms (e.g., PPA, ALM, ADMM, FISTA, etc) to approximately solve subproblems over the past two decades.

elements.sent-2411-153: In this section, we will present the application of ripALM in Algorithm \ref{algo:ripALM for solving quadratically regularized optimal transport (QROT) problems.}
elements.sent-2411-154: As a significant variant of the classical optimal transport problem, the QROT problem has garnered notable attention in recent years; see, e.g., \cite{blondel2018smooth,essid2018quadratically,lorenz2021quadratically for more details.}
elements.sent-2411-155: Mathematically, the QROT problem is given as follows: \begin{equation}\label{QROTprob} \begin{aligned} \min\limits_{X}~\Big\{\frac{\lambda}{2}\|X\|_F^2 + \langle C, \,X\rangle \; \mid\; X\bm{1}_n = \bm{\alpha}, ~~X^{\top}\bm{1}_m = \bm{\beta}, ~~X\geq0\Big\}, \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\lambda>0$ is a given regularization parameter, $C\in\mathbb{R ^{m \times n _+$ is a given cost matrix, $\bm{\alpha :=(\alpha_1,\cdots,\alpha_m)^{\top}\in\Sigma_m$ and $\bm{\beta :=(\beta_1,\cdots,\beta_n)^{\top}\in\Sigma_n$ are given probability vectors with $\Sigma_{m}$ (resp., $\Sigma_{n}$) denoting the $m$ (resp., $n$)-dimensional unit simplex, and $\bm{1 _{m}$ (resp., $\bm{1 _{n}$) denotes the $m$ (resp., $n$)-dimensional vector of all ones.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-156: To apply our proposed ripALM, we first {reformulate \eqref{QROTprob as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eq:QROTre} \begin{aligned} \min_{X\in\mathbb{R}^{m \times n}} ~\Big\{ f_{q}(X) := \frac{\lambda}{2}\left\|{X}\right\|_{F}^{2} + \left\langle {C}, {X}\right\rangle + \delta_{\mathbb{R}^{m\times n}_{+}}(X) \;\mid\; X\bm{1}_{n} = \bm{\alpha}, ~ X^{\top}\bm{1}_{m} = \bm{\beta}\Big\}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} Clearly, it falls into the form of problem \eqref{eq:mainprob (upon vectorization of $X$).}}}

elements.sent-2411-182: Based on \eqref{kkt-QROT , we define the relative KKT residual for any $(X,\,\bm{u ,\,\bm{v )$ as follows: \begin{equation}\Delta_{\rm kkt}(\bm{u},\bm{v},X) := \max\left\{\Delta_p(X), \,\Delta_d(\bm{u},\bm{v},X), \,\Delta_c(\bm{u},\bm{v},X)\right\},\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\;\Delta_p(X):= \max\left\{\frac{\|X1_{n}-\alpha\|}{1+\|\alpha\|}, \,\frac{\|X^{\top}1_{m}-\beta\|}{1+\|\beta\|}, \,\frac{\|\min\{X, \,0\}\|_F}{1+\|X\|_F}\right\}, [5pt] \; \Delta_d(u,v,X):= \frac{\|\min\{Z(u,\,v,\,X), \,0\}\|_F}{1+\|C\|_F}, \quad \;\Delta_c(u,v,X) := \frac{\left|\langle X, \,Z(u,\,v,\,X)\rangle\right|}{1+\|C\|_F}.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-183: Moreover, we define the relative duality gap as follows: \begin{equation}\Delta_{\rm gap}(\bm{u},\,\bm{v},\,X) := \cfrac{\left|\mathtt{pobj}(X) - \mathtt{dobj}(\bm{u},\bm{v})\right|}{1 + \left|\mathtt{pobj}(X)\right| + \left|\mathtt{dobj}(\bm{u},\bm{v})\right|},\end{equation} where $\mathtt{pobj (X) := f_{q (X)$ and $\mathtt{dobj (\bm{u ,\bm{v ) := - f_{q ^{*}\left(\bm{u}\bm{1 _{n ^{\top + \bm{1 _{m}\bm{v ^{\top}\right) + \bm{\alpha ^{\top}\bm{u + \bm{\beta ^{\top}\bm{v}$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-240: In this paper, we developed a relative-type inexact proximal augmented Lagrangian method (ripALM) for solving a class of linearly constrained convex optimization problems.
elements.sent-2411-241: The proposed ripALM is the first relative-type inexact version of the vanilla pALM with provable convergence guarantees.
elements.sent-2411-242: By employing a relative-type error criterion, it simplifies implementation and parameter tuning, compared to the absolute-type inexact pALM.
elements.sent-2411-243: We conducted a thorough convergence analysis and demonstrated the competitive efficiency of our ripALM through numerical experiments on solving quadratically regularized optimal transport problems.
elements.sent-2411-244: Since our algorithm is applicable to a more general linearly constrained convex optimization problem, future work may explore potential applications of this framework to other practical problems, including general regularized optimal transport problems studied in \cite{ylct2024corrected .. .}

elements.algorithm-2411-003: \caption{ADMM for solving \eqref{eq:maindual-admm2} }\label{alg:warm_start_ADMM} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE Input: a penalty parameter $\sigma>0$, and initializations $\bm{u}^{0}\in\mathbb{R}^m$, $\bm{v}^{0}\in\mathbb{R}^n$, $W^{0}, X^0\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times n}$. Set $k = 0$. \WHILE{the termination criterion is not met,} \STATE Step 1. Compute \begin{equation*} \big(\bm{u}^{k+1},\,\bm{v}^{k+1}\big) = \arg\min\limits_{\bm{u},\bm{v}}~\mathcal{L}_{\sigma}\big(\bm{u},\bm{v},W^k;X^k\big). \end{equation*} \STATE Step 2. Compute \begin{equation*} W^{k+1} = \arg\min\limits_{W} ~\mathcal{L}_{\sigma}\big(\bm{u}^{k+1},\bm{v}^{k+1},W;X^k\big). \end{equation*} \STATE Step 3. Set $X^{k+1} = X^{k} + \tau\sigma\left(\bm{u}^{k+1}\bm{1}_{n}^{\top} + \bm{1}_{m}(\bm{v}^{k+1})^{\top} - W^{k+1}\right)$, where $\tau \in \left(0, \frac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2}\right)$ is the dual step-size that is typically set to $1.618$. \STATE Step 4. Set $k \leftarrow k+1$ and go to Step 1. \ENDWHILE \STATE Output: $\big(\bm{u}^{k},\bm{v}^{k},W^{k},X^{k}\big)$. \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-355: The term ``relative" refers to controlling the errors in minimizing the subproblems based on certain computable quantities related to the current status of the algorithm.

elements.sent-2411-369: For a given penalty parameter $\sigma>0$, the augmented Lagrangian function of problem \eqref{eq:maindual is defined by (see \cite[Example 11.57]{rw1998variational ) \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \mathcal{L}_{\sigma}(\bm{y},\,\bm{x}) := \sup\limits_{\bm{s}\in\mathbb{R}^N} \left\{\ell(\bm{y},\bm{s})-\frac{1}{2\sigma}\|\bm{s}-\bm{x}\|^2\right\} = -\bm{b}^{\top}\bm{y} + \frac{1}{2\sigma}\big\|\bm{x}+\sigma{A}^{\top}\bm{y}\big\|^2 - \frac{1}{2\sigma}\|\bm{x}\|^2 - \mathtt{M}_{\sigma f}\big(\bm{x}+\sigma{A}^{\top}\bm{y}\big). \end{aligned}\end{equation} \blue{Here, we assume for the remainder of this paper that $\mathcal{L _{\sigma}$ denotes the augmented Lagrangian function of the dual problem \eqref{eq:maindual , omitting the superscript ``dual" as in \eqref{dualALMscheme .}}}}}}

elements.algorithm-2411-004: \caption{A relative-type inexact proximal augmented Lagrangian method (ripALM) for solving problem \eqref{eq:maindual}}\label{algo:ripALM} Input: \blue{$\rho\in[0,1)$, $\{\sigma_{k}\}_{k=0}^{\infty}$ such that $\inf_{k\geq0}\{\sigma_{k}\}>0$, and $\{\tau_{k}\}_{k=0}^{\infty}$ such that $0<\inf_{k\geq0}\{\tau_{k}\}\leq\sup_{k\geq0}\{\tau_{k}\}<\infty$}. Choose $\bm{y}^0,\,\bm{w}^0\in\mathbb{R}^M$ and $\bm{x}^0\in\mathbb{R}^N$ arbitrarily. Set $k=0$. while the termination criterion is not met, do \begin{itemize}[leftmargin=2.2cm] \item[Step 1.] Approximately solve the subproblem: \begin{equation}\label{ripALM-subpro} \min\limits_{\bm{y}\in\mathbb{R}^{M}}~~ \mathcal{L}_{\sigma_{k}}(\bm{y},\,\bm{x}^k) + \frac{\tau_{k}}{2\sigma_{k}}\big\|\bm{y}-\bm{y}^k\big\|^2 \end{equation} to find $\bm{y}^{k+1}\in\mathbb{R}^M$ such that \begin{equation}\label{ripALM-inexcond} \begin{aligned} \; 2\big|\langle\bm{w}^k-\bm{y}^{k+1}, \,\sigma_{k}\Delta^{k+1}\rangle\big| + \big\|\sigma_{k}\Delta^{k+1}\big\|^2 [3pt] \leq \; \rho\left( \big\|\mathtt{prox}_{\sigma_{k} f}\big(\bm{x}^k+\sigma_{k}{A}^{\top}\bm{y}^{k+1}\big)-\bm{x}^{k}\big\|^2 + \tau_{k}\big\|\bm{y}^{k+1}-\bm{y}^k\big\|^2\right), \end{aligned} \end{equation} where $\Delta^{k+1} := \nabla_{y} \mathcal{L}_{\sigma_{k}}(\bm{y}^{k+1},\bm{x}^k) + \tau_{k}\sigma_{k}^{-1}(\bm{y}^{k+1}-\bm{y}^k)$. \item[Step 2.] Update \begin{equation}\label{ripALM_xwupdate} \bm{x}^{k+1} = \mathtt{prox}_{\sigma_{k} f}\big(\bm{x}^k+\sigma_{k}{A}^{\top}\bm{y}^{k+1}\big), \quad \bm{w}^{k+1} = \bm{w}^k - \sigma_{k}\Delta^{k+1}. \end{equation} \item[Step 3.]Set $k = k+1$ and go to Step 1. \end{itemize} end while Output: $(\bm{y}^k,\bm{x}^k)$.
elements.sent-2411-372: In line with pALM-type methods, at each iteration, our ripALM in Algorithm \ref{algo:ripALM approximately minimizes the sum of the augmented Lagrangian function $\mathcal{L _{\sigma_{k (\cdot,\,\bm{x ^k)$ and a proximal term $\frac{\tau_{k {2\sigma_{k }\big\|\cdot-\bm{y ^k\big\|^2$ under the error criterion \eqref{ripALM-inexcond , followed by the updates of the multiplier $\bm{x ^k$ and the auxiliary error variable $\bm{w ^k$.}}}}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-380: Specifically, in our context, {\sc Snipal requires the error term $\Delta^{k+1}$ to satisfy \begin{equation}\label{eq:inexact_SNIPAL} \begin{aligned} (A) ~~ \big\|\Delta^{k+1}\big\| \leq \frac{\min\left(\sqrt{\tau_{k}}, \,1\right)}{\sigma_k}\varepsilon_k, ~~ \varepsilon_k\geq0, ~~ \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \varepsilon_k<\infty, (B) ~~ \big\|\Delta^{k+1}\big\| \leq \frac{\delta_k\min\left(\sqrt{\tau_{k}}, \,1\right)}{\sigma_k}\sqrt{ \big\|\Delta_x^{k+1}\big\|^2 + \tau_{k}\big\|\Delta_y^{k+1}\big\|^2}, ~~0 \leq \delta_k<1, ~~\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \delta_k<\infty, \end{aligned}\end{equation} with $\Delta_x^{k+1 :=\bm{x ^{k+1 -\bm{x ^{k}$ and $\Delta_y^{k+1 :=\bm{y ^{k+1 -\bm{y ^k$, to guarantee an asymptotic (super)linear convergence rate.\footnote{Note that the error criterion (A) in \eqref{eq:inexact_SNIPAL alone is sufficient for establishing the global convergence of the {\sc Snipal ; see \cite[Section 3]{lst2020asymptotically .}}}}}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-415: This renders the problem strongly convex while preserving the sparsity of the optimal transport plan.
elements.sent-2411-416: These favorable properties make QROT a compelling alternative to the popular entropically regularized optimal transport problem, especially in applications where the sparse structure is desirable; see, e.g., \cite{blondel2018smooth,essid2018quadratically,lorenz2021quadratically for more details.}
elements.sent-2411-417: Mathematically, the QROT problem is given as follows: \begin{equation}\label{QROTprob} \min\limits_{X}~~\frac{\lambda}{2}\|X\|_F^2 + \langle C, \,X\rangle \quad\mathrm{s.t.}\quad X\bm{1}_n = \bm{\alpha}, ~~X^{\top}\bm{1}_m = \bm{\beta}, ~~X\geq0,\end{equation} where $\lambda>0$ is a given regularization parameter, $C\in\mathbb{R ^{m \times n _+$ is a given cost matrix, $\bm{\alpha :=(\alpha_1,\cdots,\alpha_m)^{\top}\in\Sigma_m$ and $\bm{\beta :=(\beta_1,\cdots,\beta_n)^{\top}\in\Sigma_n$ are given probability vectors with $\Sigma_{m}$ (resp., $\Sigma_{n}$) denoting the $m$ (resp., $n$)-dimensional unit simplex, and $\bm{1 _{m}$ (resp., $\bm{1 _{n}$) denotes the $m$ (resp., $n$)-dimensional vector of all ones.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-418: To apply the proposed ripALM, we first reformulate \eqref{QROTprob as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eq:QROTre} \min_{X\in\mathbb{R}^{m \times n}}~~ f_{q}(X) := \frac{\lambda}{2}\left\|{X}\right\|_{F}^{2} + \left\langle {C}, {X}\right\rangle + \delta_{\mathbb{R}^{m\times n}_{+}}(X) \quad\mathrm{s.t.}\quad X\bm{1}_{n} = \bm{\alpha}, ~ X^{\top}\bm{1}_{m} = \bm{\beta}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-419: Clearly, it falls into the form of problem \eqref{eq:mainprob (upon the vectorization of $X$).}
elements.sent-2411-420: Further, one can show that the dual problem of \eqref{eq:QROTre is given by (modulo a minus sign) \begin{equation}\label{eq:dualQROT} \min\limits_{\bm{u},\,\bm{v}}~~ f_{q}^{*}\left(\bm{u}\bm{1}_{n}^{\top} + \bm{1}_{m}\bm{v}^{\top}\right) - \bm{\alpha}^{\top}\bm{u} - \bm{\beta}^{\top}\bm{v},\end{equation} and its associated augmented Lagrangian function is given by (using a similar deduction as Section \ref{sec:ripALM ) \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \mathcal{L}_{\sigma}(\bm{u},\bm{v},X) = -\bm{\alpha}^{\top}\bm{u} -\bm{\beta}^{\top}\bm{v} + \frac{1}{2\sigma}\left\|X + \sigma\left(\bm{u}\bm{1}_{n}^{\top} + \bm{1}_{m}\bm{v}^{\top}\right)\right\|_{F}^{2} - \frac{1}{2\sigma}\|X\|_{F}^{2} - \mathtt{M}_{\sigma f_{q}}\left(X + \sigma\left(\bm{u}\bm{1}_{n}^{\top} + \bm{1}_{m}\bm{v}^{\top}\right)\right), \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\bm{u \in \mathbb{R ^{m}$, $\bm{v \in \mathbb{R ^{n}$ are the Lagrange multipliers corresponding to $X\bm{1 _{n = \bm{\alpha}$ and $X^{\top}\bm{1 _{m = \bm{\beta}$, respectively, and the conjugate function $f_{q ^{*}$ admits the following expression: \begin{equation}f_{q}^{*}(Z)=\left\{ \begin{aligned} \delta_{\mathbb{R}^{m\times n}_{+}}\left(C-Z\right), \mathrm{if}~~ \lambda = 0, [3pt] \frac{1}{2\lambda}\left\|\Pi_{\mathbb{R}^{m\times n}_{+}}\left(Z - C\right)\right\|_{F}^{2}, \mathrm{if}~~ \lambda > 0. \end{aligned}\right.\end{equation}.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-421: Thus, given an arbitrary initial {guess $\left(\bm{u ^{0 ,\bm{v ^{0 , X^{0}\right) \in \mathbb{R ^{m \times \mathbb{R ^{n \times \mathbb{R ^{m\times n}$, the basic iterative scheme of our ripALM for solving \eqref{eq:dualQROT reads as follows: \begin{numcases { \left(\bm{u ^{k+1 , \bm{v ^{k+1}\right) \approx \mathop{\arg\min}\limits_{\bm{u}\in\mathbb{R ^{m ,\bm{v}\in\mathbb{R ^{n \left\{\Psi_k(\bm{u ,\bm{v )\right\}, X^{k+1 = \mathtt{prox _{\sigma_{k f_{q }\left(X^k+\sigma_{k}\left(\bm{u ^{k+1}\bm{1 _{n ^{\top + \bm{1 _{m (\bm{v ^{k+1 )^{\top}\right)\right), \end{numcases where \begin{equation}\Psi_k(\bm{u},\bm{v}):= \mathcal{L}_{\sigma_{k}}(\bm{u},\bm{v},X^k) + \frac{\tau_{k}}{2\sigma_{k}}\left\|\bm{u}-\bm{u}^k\right\|^2 + \frac{\tau_{k}}{2\sigma_{k}}\left\|\bm{v}-\bm{v}^k\right\|^2.\end{equation}.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-427: The basis pursuit denoising (BPDN) problem is a fundamental optimization problem in signal processing and compressed sensing, which aims to recover a sparse signal from noisy linear measurements \cite{van2009probing .}
elements.sent-2411-428: Specifically, given a measurement $\bm{b}\in \mathbb{R ^m$, a dictionary $D\in \mathbb{R ^{m\times n}$, and an estimate of the noise level $\widehat{\kappa >0$ in the data, the BPDN problem seeks a sparse solution $\bm{s}\in\mathbb{R ^n$ by solving the following constrained optimization problem: \begin{equation}\label{eq-bpdn} \min_{\bm{s}\in\mathbb{R}^n}~ \|\bm{s}\|_1 \quad \text{s.t.}\quad \|D\bm{s} - \bm{b}\| \leq \widehat{\kappa}.\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-429: Note that in the sparse recovery regime, one focuses on the case that $m \ll n$.
elements.sent-2411-430: By introducing an auxiliary variable $\bm{t}\in \mathbb{R ^m$, the above problem can be reformulated as \begin{equation}\label{eq-bpdn-new} \min_{\bm{s}\in\mathbb{R}^n, \,\bm{t}\in \mathbb{R}^m}\; \|\bm{s}\|_1 + \delta_{\mathcal{B}^2_{\widehat{\kappa}}}(\bm{t}) \quad \text{s.t.} \quad D\bm{s} - \bm{t} = \bm{b},\end{equation} where $\mathcal{B ^2_{\widehat{\kappa }\subset\mathbb{R ^m$ denotes the $\ell_2$-norm ball centered at $\bm{0}$ with radius $\widehat{\kappa}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-431: Then, we see that the reformulated problem \eqref{eq-bpdn-new is a special case of the general problem \eqref{eq:mainprob by letting $\bm{x :=(\bm{s ;\bm{t )\in\mathbb{R ^{n+m}$, $f(\bm{x ):= \|\bm{s}\|_1 + \delta_{\mathcal{B ^2_{\widehat{\kappa (\bm{t )$ and $A:=[D,-I]\in \mathbb{R ^{m\times (n+m)}$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-432: Consequently, the proposed ripALM is directly applicable to solve the BPDN problem.

elements.sent-2411-532: In this section, we present the alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMM, see, e.g. \cite{boyd2011distributed,gabay1976dual ) for solving the dual problem \eqref{eq:dualQROT , which can be reformulated as: \begin{equation}\label{eq:maindual-admm2} \begin{aligned} \min\limits_{\bm{u}\in\mathbb{R}^m,\,\bm{v}\in\mathbb{R}^n,\,W\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times n}} \Big\{ f_{q}^{*}(W) - \bm{\alpha}^{\top}\bm{u} - \bm{\beta}^{\top}\bm{v} \;\mid\; \bm{u}\bm{1}_{n}^{\top} + \bm{1}_{m}\bm{v}^{\top} = W\Big\}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} Given a penalty parameter $\sigma > 0$, the augmented Lagrangian function of \eqref{eq:maindual-admm2 is \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \; \mathcal{L}_{\sigma}\left(\bm{u},\bm{v},W;X\right) := \; f_{q}^{*}(W) - \bm{\alpha}^{\top}\bm{u} - \bm{\beta}^{\top}\bm{v} + \big\langle X, \,\bm{u}\bm{1}_{n}^{\top}+\bm{1}_{m}\bm{v}^{\top}-W\big\rangle + \frac{\sigma}{2}\big\| \bm{u}\bm{1}_{n}^{\top} + \bm{1}_{m}\bm{v}^{\top}-W\big\|_F^{2}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} Then, the ADMM for solving \eqref{eq:maindual-admm2 can be described as in Algorithm \ref{alg:warm_start_ADMM .}}}}}
elements.algorithm-2411-005: \caption{ADMM for solving \eqref{eq:maindual-admm2}}\label{alg:warm_start_ADMM} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE Input: a penalty parameter $\sigma>0$, and initializations $\bm{u}^{0}\in\mathbb{R}^m$, $\bm{v}^{0}\in\mathbb{R}^n$, $W^{0}, X^0\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times n}$. Set $k = 0$. \WHILE{the termination criterion is not met,} \STATE Step 1. Compute \begin{equation*} \big(\bm{u}^{k+1},\,\bm{v}^{k+1}\big) =\arg\min\limits_{\bm{u},\bm{v}}~\mathcal{L}_{\sigma}\big(\bm{u},\bm{v},W^k;X^k\big). \end{equation*} \STATE Step 2. Compute \begin{equation*} W^{k+1} = \arg\min\limits_{W} ~\mathcal{L}_{\sigma}\big(\bm{u}^{k+1},\bm{v}^{k+1},W;X^k\big). \end{equation*} \STATE Step 3. elements.section-2411-013: Efficient implementation of ripALM for BPDN
elements.sent-2411-535: When applying ripALM to the BPDN problem \eqref{eq-bpdn , a key component is the efficient implementation of the semismooth Newton ({\sc Ssn ) method for solving the non-smooth equation \eqref{eq:optcond-y .}}}
elements.sent-2411-536: The main computational task in {\sc Ssn lies in computing the Newton direction, which requires solving the linear system \eqref{eq:gen_dir_Newton-BPDN .}}
elements.sent-2411-537: In the following, we describe how this system can be solved efficiently.
elements.sent-2411-539: We restate \eqref{eq:gen_dir_Newton-BPDN as follows \begin{equation}\label{eq:gen_dir_Newton_2} H\bm{d} = - \bm{g},\end{equation} where $\bm{g := \nabla\Phi(\bm{y )$, and $H\in\widehat{\partial (\nabla\Phi)(\bm{y )$ has the form \begin{equation}H := \sigma\left(DUD^{\top} + V\right) + \frac{\tau}{\sigma}I_{m},\end{equation} with ${U \in \partial \mathtt{prox _{\sigma\|\cdot\|_1 (\bm{u )$, $\bm{u := \bar{\bm{s +\sigma {D ^{\top}\bm{y}$, and $V \in \partial \Pi_{\mathcal{B _{\widehat{\kappa ^2 (\bm{v )$, $\bm{v := \bar{\bm{t -\sigma \bm{y}$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-540: Moreover, the generalized Jacobians $U$ and $V$ are chosen as follows: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} U = \; \text{Diag}(\bm{w}) ~~\text{with}~~w_{i} = \left\{\begin{array}{l} 0, \quad \text{if}~ |u_{i}| \leq \sigma, 1, \quad \text {otherwise, } \end{array}\right.,~~ i = 1,\dots, n, V = \;\left\{\begin{array}{ll} I_{m}, \|\bm{v}\| \leq \widehat{\kappa}, \frac{\widehat{\kappa}}{\|\bm{v}\|}\left(I_{m}-\frac{1}{\|\bm{v}\|^2}\bm{v} \bm{v}^{\top}\right), \|\bm{v}\| > \widehat{\kappa}. \end{array}\right. \end{aligned}\end{equation} Thanks to the special structure of $H$, we can solve the linear system \eqref{eq:gen_dir_Newton_2 efficiently by considering the following two cases.. Case I.}}}
elements.formula-2411-137: \begin{equation} U = \; Diag(w) ~~with~~w_{i} = \left\{{l} 0, \quad if~ |u_{i}| \leq \sigma, 1, \quad \text {otherwise, } \right.,~~ i = 1,\dots, n, V = \;\left\{{ll} I_{m}, \|v\| \leq \widehat{\kappa}, \frac{\widehat{\kappa}}{\|v\|}\left(I_{m}-\frac{1}{\|v\|^2}v v^{\top}\right), \|v\| > \widehat{\kappa}. \right. }}\end{equation}

elements.sent-2411-545: To solve \eqref{eq:gen_dir_Newton_BPDN_1_reduced , we proceed as follows: When $m\leq|\mathcal{I |$, we directly factorize the coefficient matrix $D_{\mathcal{I D_{\mathcal{I ^{\top +\bar{\gamma I_{m}$, which requires $O(m^3)$ arithmetic operations.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-546: Otherwise, if $m>|\mathcal{I |$, we can utilize the Sherman--Morrison--Woodbury (SMW) formula to improve efficiency.}

elements.sent-2411-550: In this case, the linear system \eqref{eq:gen_dir_Newton_2 reduces to \begin{equation}\label{eq-coe-mat-c2} \left(B+D_{\mathcal{I}}D_{\mathcal{I}}^{\top}\right)\bm{d} = -\frac{1}{\sigma}\bm{g} \quad\text{with}\quad B:= \left(\frac{\widehat{\kappa}}{\|\bm{v}\|}+\frac{\tau}{\sigma^2}\right)I_{m} - \frac{\widehat{\kappa}}{\|\bm{v}\|^3}\bm{v}\bm{v}^{\top},\end{equation} which is more complicated than Case I due to the presence of the dense matrix $\bm{v}\bm{v ^{\top}$.}}

elements.sent-2411-555: Finally, we mention that when direct factorization of the above matrices is computationally prohibitive, one may instead apply an iterative method—such as the preconditioned conjugate gradient method—to solve the linear system \eqref{eq:gen_dir_Newton_2 .}

elements.sent-2411-557: In this section, we briefly discuss how to apply ADMM for solving the dual problem \eqref{eq:BPDN-dual of the BPDN problem.}
elements.sent-2411-558: To this end, we first reformulate the dual problem as: \begin{equation}\label{eq:BPDN-dadmm} \begin{aligned} \min_{\bm{u},\,\bm{v},\,\bm{y}} \quad \delta_{\mathcal{B}_1^\infty}(\bm{u}) + \widehat{\kappa}\|\bm{v}\| - \bm{b}^{\top}\bm{y} \mathrm{s.t.} ~ \quad D^{\top}\bm{y} - \bm{u} = 0, ~ -\bm{y} - \bm{v} = 0. \end{aligned}\end{equation} Given a penalty parameter $\sigma >0$, the augmented Lagrangian function of \eqref{eq:BPDN-dadmm is \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \mathcal{L}_{\sigma}(\bm{u}, \bm{v}, \bm{y}; \bm{s}, \bm{t}) := ~ \delta_{\mathcal{B}_1^\infty}(\bm{u}) + \widehat{\kappa}\|\bm{v}\| - \bm{b}^{\top}\bm{y} + \langle \bm{s}, \,D^{\top}\bm{y} - \bm{u}\rangle + \langle \bm{t}, \,-\bm{y} - \bm{v}\rangle + {\textstyle\frac{\sigma}{2}\left\|D^{\top}\bm{y} - \bm{u}\right\|^2 + \frac{\sigma}{2}\left\|-\bm{y} - \bm{v}\right\|^2}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} The ADMM for solving \eqref{eq:BPDN-dadmm is presented in Algorithm~\ref{alg:BPDN-dadmm .}}}
elements.formula-2411-145: \begin{equation} L_{\sigma}(u, v, y; s, t) := ~ \delta_{B_1^\infty}(u) + \widehat{\kappa}\|v\| - b^{\top}y + \langle s, \,D^{\top}y - u\rangle + \langle t, \,-y - v\rangle + {\textstyle\frac{\sigma}{2}\left\|D^{\top}y - u\right\|^2 + \frac{\sigma}{2}\left\|-y - v\right\|^2}. \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-559: In Step 1, we can obtain the closed-form solution to the subproblem by evaluating the proximal mappings of $\delta_{\mathcal{B _1^\infty (\cdot)$ and $\frac{\widehat{\kappa {\sigma}\|\cdot\|$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-560: In Step 2, the subproblem reduces to solving a linear system, where the coefficient matrix $I + DD^\top$ is symmetric positive definite and remains unchanged throughout the iterations, and therefore requires factorization only once.
elements.sent-2411-561: For large-scale problems where such a factorization is prohibited, one may also apply the preconditioned conjugate gradient method for solving the linear system.
elements.algorithm-2411-006: \caption{ADMM for solving \eqref{eq:BPDN-dadmm}}\label{alg:BPDN-dadmm} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE Input: a penalty parameter $\sigma>0$, and initializations $\bm{y}^{0}\in\mathbb{R}^{m}$, $\bm{s}^0\in\mathbb{R}^{n}$, $\bm{t}^{0}\in\mathbb{R}^{m}$. \WHILE{the termination criterion is not met} \STATE Step 1. Compute \begin{equation*} \begin{aligned} \;\big(\bm{u}^{k+1},\,\bm{v}^{k+1}\big) = \arg\min_{\bm{u},\bm{v}}\left\{\mathcal{L}_{\sigma}(\bm{u}, \bm{v},\bm{y}^{k}; \bm{s}^{k}, \bm{t}^{k})\right\} [2pt] \Leftrightarrow~~ \; \bm{u}^{k+1} = \Pi_{\mathcal{B}_1^\infty}\left(D^\top\bm{y}^k + \sigma^{-1}\bm{s}^k\right),\quad \bm{v}^{k+1} = \sigma^{-1}\left(\left(\bm{t}^{k} - \sigma\bm{y}^{k}\right) - \Pi_{{\mathcal{B}_{\widehat{\kappa}}^2}}\left(\bm{t}^{k} - \sigma\bm{y}^{k}\right)\right). \end{aligned} \end{equation*} \STATE Step 2. Compute \begin{equation*} \begin{aligned} \; \bm{y}^{k+1} = \arg\min_{\bm{y}\in\mathbb{R}^{m}} \left\{\mathcal{L}_{\sigma}(\bm{u}^{k+1},\bm{v}^{k+1},\bm{y};\bm{s}^{k},\bm{t}^{k})\right\} \Leftrightarrow~~ \; \big(DD^{\top} + I\big)\bm{y}^{k+1} =\sigma^{-1}\left(\bm{b} - D\big(\bm{s}^{k} - \sigma\bm{u}^{k+1}\big) + \big(\bm{t}^{k} - \sigma\bm{v}^{k+1}\big)\right). \end{aligned} \end{equation*} \STATE Step 3. Compute \begin{equation*} \bm{s}^{k+1} = \bm{s}^{k} + \tau\sigma\big({D}^{\top}\bm{y}^{k+1} - \bm{u}^{k+1}\big), \quad \bm{t}^{k+1} = \bm{t}^{k} + \tau\sigma\big(-\bm{y}^{k+1} - \bm{v}^{k+1}\big), \end{equation*} where $\tau \in \left(0, \,\frac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2}\right)$ is the dual step-size that is typically set to $1.618$. \ENDWHILE \STATE Output: $\left(\bm{s}^{k+1},\,\bm{t}^{k+1},\,\bm{y}^{k+1}\right)$. \end{algorithmic}
elements.section-2411-015: Second-order cone programming reformulation for BPDN
elements.sent-2411-562: In this section, we present a second-order cone programming (SOCP) reformulation of the BPDN problem \eqref{eq-bpdn .}
elements.sent-2411-563: Specifically, we introduce an auxiliary nonnegative variable $\bm{r}\in\mathbb{R ^n_+$ to majorize $|\bm{s |$, i.e., $|\bm{s |\leq\bm{r}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-564: Thus, the objective $\|\bm{s}\|_1$ can be replaced by $\bm{1 ^{\top}\bm{r}$ together with the linear constraints $-\bm{r}\leq \bm{s \leq\bm{r}$ and $\bm{r \geq \bm{0}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-565: Next, for a given positive integer $d$, we denote the $(d+1)$-dimensional second-order cone as $\mathcal{Q ^{d+1 := \big\{ (s_0, \bm{s )\in\mathbb{R ^{d+1 \mid s_0 \geq \|\bm{s}\|\big\}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-566: With these notations, the BPDN problem \eqref{eq-bpdn admits the following SOCP reformulation: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \min_{\bm{s},\,\bm{r}\in\mathbb{R}^{n}} \quad \bm{1}^{\top}\bm{r} \mathrm{ s.t. } ~ \quad -\bm{r}\leq \bm{s} \leq\bm{r}, \quad \bm{r} \geq \bm{0}, \quad \big(\widehat{\kappa}, D\bm{s}-\bm{b}\big) \in \mathcal{Q}^{m+1}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} \color{black \bibliographystyle{plain.}}}
